question: Distinguish precisely among Starlink Subscriber, Service Line, and supported accounts. Which is most analogous to a revenue-generating customer, which has the highest count, and why?
facts: A Service Line is defined as an individual instance of Starlink broadband internet service provisioned under a subscription plan, generally associated with a specific Starlink User Terminal or group of terminals, and billed according to Starlink's service plans and terms of service.
A Service Line is explicitly not equivalent to a unique device, account holder, end user, or physical person.

A Starlink Subscriber is defined as a unique Service Line that is directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity that does not have a direct, negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team.
Starlink Subscribers include Personal subscriptions (Residential, Roam) and Business subscriptions (Local Priority, Global Priority).
Starlink Subscribers explicitly exclude managed enterprise and government customers with negotiated contracts in aviation, maritime, land mobility, fixed sites, and government entities.
The Starlink Subscriber count was approximately 10.3 million as of March 31, 2026.
The total number of Service Lines across all of Starlink is necessarily higher than the Starlink Subscriber count.
A Supported Account is defined as a human, bot, or similar account that logged into the X platform or Grok.
Supported Accounts may include fake, spam, or bot accounts if they are active.
The Supported Account count was over 1.3 billion for the last twelve months ended March 31, 2026.
Starlink Subscriber is the most analogous to a revenue-generating customer.
Each Starlink Subscriber represents a unique, actively billed subscription line generating recurring monthly service revenue.
Consumer subscribers represented over 60% of Connectivity segment revenue in 2025.
Supported Accounts have the highest count at approximately 1.3 billion.
Supported Accounts exceed Starlink Subscribers by approximately 126x.

Supported Accounts have the highest count because they measure any login event on a global social media and AI platform over a twelve-month window with no quality filter, whereas Starlink Subscribers measure only paying broadband subscriptions with hardware requirements that explicitly exclude the enterprise/government segment.

question: What specific language does the filing use regarding timing of Starship commercial payload delivery, V3 satellite deployment, and V2 Mobile deployment? Compare the wording and assess what it suggests about execution and timing risk.
facts: The filing states regarding Starship commercial payload delivery: "We expect Starship to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026."
The filing states regarding Starship commercial payload delivery: "We expect Starship to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026 following additional flight tests."

The filing states regarding V3 satellite deployment: "We expect to commence deploying our next-generation V3 satellites, designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite, using Starship in the second half of 2026."

The filing states regarding V3 satellite deployment: "...and in the second half of 2026, we expect to begin deployment of our next-generation V3 satellites, each of which is designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite."

The filing states regarding V2 Mobile satellite deployment: "We plan to expand our mobile constellation by deploying our next-generation mobile V2 Mobile satellites in 2027 which, combined with the EchoStar spectrum acquisition and optimized 5G protocols, are expected to increase capacity by orders of magnitude compared to our first-generation constellation."
The filing states: "Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites."
The filing states: "The core V3 satellite design is complete."
The filing states: "We expect the spectrum acquisition to close in November 2027, subject to required regulatory approvals and other closing conditions."

The filing states: "...we expect to receive the remaining necessary U.S. regulatory authorizations in the second or third quarter of 2026...we still require our V2 Mobile satellites to be in orbit and must complete the acquisition of the relevant spectrum from EchoStar before we can commence our planned commercial Gen2 service in the United States."

The filing states: "If we are unable to achieve the commercial development, anticipated performance, launch cadence, or cost efficiencies associated with Starship within expected timeframes, our ability to deploy next-generation V3 satellites, V2 Mobile satellites, and orbital AI compute infrastructure at scale...could be materially and adversely affected."

The filing states: "Achieving our targeted launch cadence with Starship will require significant progress on several key milestones...and obtaining the necessary regulatory approvals, particularly from the FAA."
The filing states: "Delays or challenges in the Starship program have in the past occurred, and may occur in the future."

The filing states: "Full reusability of Starship's upper stage is not required to deploy our V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites in low-Earth orbit...Starship's substantial payload capacity to LEO, even in partially reusable or expendable configurations, enables meaningful progress toward these objectives."
Starship payload delivery and V3 deployment use the confidence verb "expect," while V2 Mobile deployment uses the weaker confidence verb "plan."
Starship payload delivery and V3 deployment use the action verbs "commence" or "begin," while V2 Mobile deployment uses "deploying" without a "commence" qualifier.
Starship payload delivery and V3 deployment specify a 6-month timeframe ("second half of 2026"), while V2 Mobile deployment specifies a 12-month timeframe ("2027").
Starship payload delivery has 1 critical-path dependency (additional flight tests).
V3 satellite deployment has 2 critical-path dependencies (Starship operational readiness and satellite manufacturing).
V2 Mobile deployment has 3 or more critical-path dependencies (Starship readiness, EchoStar spectrum acquisition, and regulatory approvals).
All three milestones are structurally dependent on Starship with no fallback launch vehicle.
The shift from "expect" to "plan" signals an intention that is more contingent on external factors partially outside SpaceX's direct control.
The use of "commence" or "begin" signals only the initiation of operations, not commercial scale, regular cadence, or revenue materiality.
V2 Mobile faces the highest timing risk due to compounding execution risk from three independent critical-path dependencies.
Commercial Gen2 service cannot begin until late 2027 at the earliest, and only if all three V2 Mobile dependencies are simultaneously satisfied.
V3 and V2 Mobile deployment could still proceed at higher per-launch cost even if full Starship reusability is delayed.

The filing's language constructs a hierarchy of timing confidence: Starship payload delivery and V3 deployment are presented with roughly equivalent moderate confidence as near-term expectations for H2 2026, while V2 Mobile is presented with lower confidence as a plan for 2027 with materially more external dependencies.
The filing's own risk disclosures acknowledge that delays have in the past occurred and could cascade across the entire growth strategy.
The timelines should be interpreted as aspirational targets subject to significant execution risk, not as committed delivery dates.

question: Assume a hypothetical year in which orbital AI compute satellite manufacturing costs total $2B. Quantify the difference in AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA between capitalization and expensing treatment and identify which disclosed accounting policies would govern the judgment.
facts: The capitalization treatment produces AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA that is $2.00 billion higher than the expensing treatment in the hypothetical year.

Segment Adjusted EBITDA = Income (Loss) from Operations + Depreciation & Amortization + Share-Based Compensation + Restructuring Charges + Impairment.
Under capitalization treatment, the net impact on Segment Adjusted EBITDA is $0.
Under expensing treatment, the net impact on Segment Adjusted EBITDA is −$2B.
The R&D expensing policy requires the Company to expense all R&D costs as they are incurred.
The Starship commercialization precedent establishes a bright-line commercialization milestone, requiring costs to be expensed as R&D until a commercialization threshold is met.
The PP&E / Satellite capitalization policy states that capitalized satellite costs include direct manufacturing costs (parts, labor, and allocated overhead) as well as capitalized launch costs.

The inter-segment cost allocation policy states that when the Space segment launches the company's own satellites, no inter-segment revenue is recognized and all associated launch costs are directly capitalized into the end-user segment's PP&E.
The interest capitalization policy states that the Company capitalizes interest costs associated with significant, long-term construction projects.
The central accounting judgment governing the treatment is whether orbital AI compute satellite manufacturing costs are in the development/R&D stage or the production/commercial stage.
The R&D expensing policy and Starship commercialization precedent would most likely govern in the near term, requiring the $2B to be expensed.
